gloss: Negation, absence, the unconditioned — classically a bare negative particle, in Chan/Zen a koan object that points past discursive mind.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A person with arms outstretched holding what appear to be ornamental tassels or dance props — a pictograph of ritual dance
graphic_evolution.notes: Originally depicted a dancer in ceremonial performance. The semantic shift from 'dancer' to 'negation/non-existence' is one of the most dramatic in classical Chinese — the character was phonetically borrowed for the negative particle and its pictographic meaning abandoned entirely.

graphic_evolution.form_description: The dancing figure becomes more abstract; the tassels formalize into symmetrical downward strokes
graphic_evolution.notes: By the Qin standardization the pictographic origin is mostly obscured, though the bilateral symmetry of the original dance posture survives in the balanced structure.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Four dots at the bottom (simplified flame-strokes from 火 radical) with a complex upper structure
graphic_evolution.notes: The 火 radical in the modern form is a graphic accident of simplification, not a semantic clue. Do not read 無 as having anything to do with fire — the radical assignment is taxonomic convention, not etymology.

doctrinal_notes: In Chan/Zen, 無 functions primarily as a koan object (話頭, huatou) rather than a philosophical concept. Zhaozhou's 無 in the Gateless Gate Case 1 is the most widely assigned koan in Rinzai training. The character is often written in large calligraphic form and displayed in Zen practice halls.
japanese_transmission: Present in Japanese literate culture from the first-wave adoption of Chinese writing in the 5th–6th centuries, where it functioned as an ordinary classical-Chinese negative. Its load-bearing sense arrives much later, with the transmission of Chan in the Kamakura period (late 12th–13th centuries): Eisai and then Dōgen bring the Song-dynasty koan curriculum, and Zhaozhou's 無 enters Japanese Rinzai training as the first assigned huatou. The character's status as koan-object — rather than philosophical concept — is entirely a Kamakura-onward development in Japanese.

gloss: A hollow, empty space — the positive name for śūnyatā: phenomena empty of inherent self-nature, yet fully present.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A cave or pit opening (穴) over a craft/work element (工)
graphic_evolution.notes: The pictographic logic is visible: an opening worked into the earth, a hollow space. The cave radical 穴 is clearly an arched opening; 工 functions as phonetic but the semantic resonance of 'crafted emptiness' was noted by classical commentators.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 穴 (cave, 5 strokes) over 工 (work, 3 strokes) — the modern form preserves the original composition clearly
graphic_evolution.notes: One of the more transparent characters in Buddhist vocabulary — the composition still legibly depicts a hollow space. The sky meaning (そら) preserves the image of open space above.

doctrinal_notes: The primary term for śūnyatā in East Asian Buddhism. Appears in the Heart Sutra's most famous line (色即是空). Distinct from 無 in that 空 is a positive characterization of the nature of phenomena (they are empty of inherent existence) rather than a bare negative.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the Nara-period (710–794) transmission of the six schools of Chinese Buddhism, and specifically with the Sanron (Madhyamaka) school founded at temples like Gangōji. The Heart Sutra, whose pivotal formula 色即是空 makes 空 the compact emblem of Prajñāpāramitā doctrine, is part of the core liturgy from the Nara period onward and is still chanted daily in most Japanese Buddhist schools. The kun reading そら (sky) belongs to the older native Japanese layer and was mapped onto the imported character when the writing system was being fixed.

gloss: Heart-mind — the undivided seat of feeling, thought, and moral faculty; in Chan/Zen identified directly with Buddha-nature.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An anatomical heart with chambers and descending vessels — clearly pictographic
graphic_evolution.notes: One of the most recognizable oracle bone pictographs. The ancient form shows the heart as a physical organ with its structural features visible. Seeing this form makes the abstract modern character suddenly legible as what it has always been: a heart.

graphic_evolution.form_description: The anatomical detail elaborates slightly; the chambers become more defined
graphic_evolution.notes: The bronze form is often more ornate than oracle bone. The heart shape is still clearly legible.

graphic_evolution.form_description: The pictograph begins abstracting — the chambers become three curved strokes, the vessels a downward element
graphic_evolution.notes: The transition from icon to abstract form is underway. The three 'bumps' of the modern character begin to emerge from what were the heart's chambers.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Four strokes: a leftward hook, two short interior strokes, and a rightward dot — fully abstract
graphic_evolution.notes: The modern form retains none of the pictographic legibility of the oracle bone form. But the stroke count (4) is low enough that 心 as a radical is easy to recognize in compound characters: 忘 (forget), 想 (think), 悟 (awaken) all carry 心 at the bottom.

doctrinal_notes: The most philosophically loaded character in East Asian Buddhist and Confucian thought. Functions simultaneously as the physical heart, the seat of consciousness, the moral faculty, and the ground of awakening. Chan/Zen teaching often equates 心 with Buddha-nature directly.
japanese_transmission: The native Japanese word こころ predates writing; the character 心 was mapped onto it in the first-wave adoption of Chinese script (5th–6th centuries), via Korean scribes in the Yamato court. Its philosophical weight accretes in layers: first with Nara-period Buddhism (心 as citta in Yogācāra and Abhidharma texts), then with Heian Tendai and Shingon, and most consequentially with Kamakura Zen, where 即心是仏 (this very mind is Buddha) becomes a stock teaching formula. The Confucian heart-mind register arrives later still, with the Tokugawa (1603–1868) establishment of Neo-Confucianism as official doctrine.

gloss: A way walked — road, route, and by extension the cosmic ordering principle, the moral path of self-cultivation, and the Buddhist mārga, all at once.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A crossroads (行) with a head (首) — a figure at a junction, oriented and moving
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image: a person's head at a crossroads, suggesting oriented movement through space. The 'way' as something walked, a route taken, not an abstract principle — the abstract meaning develops from this concrete image.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 首 (head) over 辵 (movement/foot) — the semantic components are now clearly legible
graphic_evolution.notes: The Qin standardization preserves both components: 首 (head, direction, what leads) and 辵 (a foot in motion, going). The combination: directed movement, a head leading a body along a way.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 辶 (movement radical, simplified from 辵) wrapping around 首 — 13 strokes
graphic_evolution.notes: The movement radical 辶 is the cursive simplification of 辵. It appears in many characters involving movement, path, or going: 近 (near), 遠 (far), 遊 (wander). 道 among them carries the heaviest philosophical freight.

doctrinal_notes: Carries simultaneous Daoist (cosmic ordering principle), Confucian (moral path of self-cultivation), and Buddhist (mārga, path of practice) resonances. Chan teaching exploits all three registers, most explicitly in the formula 平常心是道 (ordinary mind is the Way).
japanese_transmission: Like 心, 道 maps an existing native word (みち, 'path, road') onto an imported character in the first-wave Chinese script adoption. The philosophical freight accumulates over centuries: Daoist and Confucian readings from Nara-period classical study, Buddhist mārga from Nara and Heian scholasticism, Zen's full exploitation of all three registers in the Kamakura period. The productive Japanese suffix -dō (柔道, 茶道, 剣道, 書道) — a discipline pursued as a total way of life — is a late-medieval and Tokugawa development that repurposes the character's philosophical density into a framework for cultivated practice.

gloss: Sagely discernment — the clarity of one whose speech cuts through to the essential; recruited by Nishi Amane to name the Western discipline of philosophy.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 折 (to break, to fold — phonetic) over 口 (mouth) — a mouth that cuts through to clarity
graphic_evolution.notes: The phonetic component 折 (zhé) provides the pronunciation; 口 (mouth/speech) provides the semantic direction. The combination suggests someone whose speech cuts through to the essential point — a sage whose words illuminate.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 折 (10 strokes reduced) over 口 — the modern form
graphic_evolution.notes: Used in classical Chinese for a person of exceptional sagely insight. Nishi Amane's choice of 哲 for 'philosophy' frames Western inquiry as a form of sage-wisdom cultivation — a framing with significant consequences for how East Asian intellectuals received Western philosophy.

doctrinal_notes: Classical term for a sage or wise person of discernment (哲人, tetsujin). Recruited by Nishi Amane in 1874 to translate Western 'philosophy' — the study of 哲 wisdom. The coinage 哲学 remains universal in Japanese and Chinese to this day.
japanese_transmission: Present in Japan from the classical period as a rare literary character in Chinese texts — the kind a Nara or Heian-period court scholar would encounter reading the Book of Documents or Analects commentaries, but not a working part of Japanese vocabulary. Its consequential arrival is 1874, when Nishi Amane (西周) picks 哲 as the first character of 哲学 to render Western 'philosophy' in Hyakuichi Shinron. Overnight a classical literary rarity becomes the basis for the academic vocabulary of an entire discipline.

gloss: Learning received and transmitted — originally the image of adult hands passing knowledge to a child; the workhorse suffix for every Meiji-coined academic discipline.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Hands passing an object to a child (子) — the image of instruction, transmission of knowledge
graphic_evolution.notes: The original pictograph shows adult hands (above) transmitting something to a child (below). Learning as receiving from those who came before — the Confucian model of education as transmission is built into the character's origin.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A complex upper element (covering/receiving hands) over 子 (child) — 8 strokes in simplified form
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplified form (学 vs traditional 學) drops much of the upper complexity. 子 at the bottom remains clear. Functions as the standard suffix for academic disciplines: 哲学, 科学, 文学, 医学 — philosophy, science, literature, medicine.

doctrinal_notes: As a suffix (学, -gaku/-xué), 学 is the workhorse of Meiji academic vocabulary. Nearly every Western disciplinary name was coined by appending 学 to a characterization of the field's subject matter. This suffix pattern made the vocabulary systematic and extensible.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy — the Nara-period court's absorption of Confucian texts made 学 the basic word for 'learning, study.' It deepens as an institutional term through the Heian daigakuryō (imperial academy) and the Tokugawa expansion of domain schools and private academies. Its most consequential reuse is Meiji: the 1870s–80s systematize 学 as the standard suffix for Western disciplines (哲学, 科学, 文学, 医学), and this suffix pattern then transfers into Chinese, reshaping the academic vocabulary of both languages.

gloss: A shrine community, then any organized association — from the local earth-deity gathering to the modern corporate body.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 示 (altar/spirit) beside 土 (earth) — a shrine to the earth deity
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning: a shrine to the local earth spirit (社神, the deity of a specific place and its community). The religious-communal gathering around a local shrine is the semantic root of 社's extension to 'association' and 'company.'

graphic_evolution.form_description: 示 (spirit/altar radical, 4 strokes) over 土 (earth, 3 strokes) — 7 strokes
graphic_evolution.notes: The earth shrine meaning gives way to 'organized association' in classical usage (社団, 会社). Meiji coiners recruited this corporate/associative sense to render 'society' as an organized collective.

doctrinal_notes: In 社会 (society), 社 contributes the sense of organized association or corporate body — a group gathered around a common purpose or place, extended from the original shrine community. The character also appears in 会社 (company/corporation) and 神社 (Shinto shrine).
japanese_transmission: Arrives with first-wave Chinese script but is absorbed into the specifically Japanese religious landscape through the compound 神社 (jinja), applied to Shintō shrines from at least the Nara period onward. The kun reading やしろ is the older native word for a shrine precinct, mapped onto the imported character. The abstract 'association / corporate body' sense — already present in classical Chinese usage — is reactivated in the Meiji period to construct the modern sociological vocabulary: 社会 (society), 会社 (company), 社説 (editorial).

gloss: Gathering, meeting, convergence — things and people coming together under a shared occasion.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A covered vessel or lid over a mouth/opening — things coming together under a cover
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image suggests convergence: things coming together, fitting together, meeting. Extended to human assembly — people gathering, meeting, converging.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 人 (person) element over a joining element — 6 strokes in simplified form (traditional 會 is more complex)
graphic_evolution.notes: Traditional 會 is 13 strokes; simplified 会 is 6. The simplification loses some of the visual complexity but the 'coming together' meaning is preserved in usage: 会議 (meeting), 会社 (company), 社会 (society).

doctrinal_notes: In 社会 (society), 会 contributes the sense of gathering, assembly, coming together. The compound 社会 thus suggests an organized community gathering — an association of people. The abstract sociological sense (an autonomous collective sphere) is what the Meiji coinage imported from the Western concept.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from Nara times in its traditional form 會. It carries a strong Buddhist liturgical register throughout the classical and medieval periods — 法会 (hōe, Dharma assembly) is a standard term in temple records from at least the 9th century. The Meiji period mobilizes its 'gathering, assembly' sense for the new vocabulary of civil association (会議, 会社, 社会, 議会), and the postwar script reform (1946) replaces traditional 會 with simplified 会, which is now universal in Japanese print.

gloss: The people — classically the governed, positioned below the ruler; repurposed at Meiji to name the sovereign source of political authority.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Disputed — possibly a blinded eye (captives blinded) or a person kneeling
graphic_evolution.notes: The oracle bone form's interpretation is contested. One reading sees a blinded left eye — captives taken in battle were sometimes blinded, and 民 may have originally meant 'captive/subject people.' This etymology, if correct, encodes a relationship of subjection from the origin.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 5 strokes — a simple form with no obvious pictographic logic in the modern form
graphic_evolution.notes: In classical Chinese political vocabulary, 民 (the people) is consistently positioned below the ruler (君, 王) and above nothing. The Confucian ideal: the ruler exists for the people, but the people do not rule. This is the classical charge that 民主 (democracy) inverts.

doctrinal_notes: In 民主 (democracy) and 民主主義 (democracy as doctrine), 民 is the political subject being redefined — from governed people to sovereign people. The classical resonance of 民 as the governed, the subjects, creates a latent tension with the democratic claim that 民 are the source of sovereign authority.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese word たみ (the people, the populace) in the earliest stratum of Japanese writing. Through the classical and medieval periods it carries its inherited Confucian valence — the governed, positioned below the ruler. Its modern political load is Meiji-era: Fukuzawa Yukichi and other translators repurpose 民 and the classical compound 民主 ('the people's lord') to render the vocabulary of Western liberal-democratic thought, inverting the classical relationship so that 民 names the sovereign rather than the governed. The tension between the two senses has remained productive in East Asian political discourse ever since.

gloss: Lord, master, the central one — the lamp flame around which a space is oriented; doubles as the leading character of 主義, the suffix for every -ism.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A lamp or torch with a flame — the flame at the center, the light that governs a space
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image: a lamp flame, the central light source that orients everything around it. Extended to: the one who is central, who governs, who is the master or lord of a domain.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 5 strokes — a vertical stroke with a dot at top and a horizontal base
graphic_evolution.notes: The flame has become fully abstract. 主 appears twice in 民主主義: first as 'lord/master' (in 民主), then as the first character of 主義 (-ism, guiding principle). The double appearance is a structural accident of Chinese compounding.

doctrinal_notes: Classical meaning: lord, master, the one who rules or owns. In 民主, literally 'the people's lord' or 'lord of the people' — a paternalistic Confucian construction that Meiji coiners repurposed to mean 'rule by the people.' The inversion is built into the characters.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese words ぬし (owner, lord of a place) and おも (principal, main). Medieval Japanese uses 主 freely for temple abbots, estate holders, and domain lords; the Christian mission of the 16th century also presses it into service for 'Lord' in its theological sense (天主). Its Meiji-era repurposing is twofold: it does double duty in 民主主義, first as 'lord' (in 民主) and then as the leading character of 主義 (-ism), the systematized suffix for Western doctrines. The coincidence is a structural feature of how Meiji translators built Japanese political vocabulary out of classical Chinese compounding.

gloss: Disciplinary category — measuring out grain, then sorting knowledge into fields; the bureaucratic lineage behind the Meiji coinage of 'science'.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 禾 (grain/rice plant) beside 斗 (measuring scoop) — measuring out grain
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning: to measure out grain with a scoop. Extended to: classification, category, division — the act of sorting and measuring extended to the sorting of knowledge into categories.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 禾 (grain radical, 5 strokes) beside 斗 (dipper, 4 strokes) — 9 strokes
graphic_evolution.notes: The measuring/classification meaning survives in the imperial examination system: 科挙 (keju) — examinations organized by subject category. This is the background Nishi Amane drew on when coining 科学: science as knowledge organized by disciplinary categories.

doctrinal_notes: In 科学 (science), 科 contributes the sense of disciplinary categorization — knowledge sorted into organized fields. The examination system legacy means 科 implicitly frames knowledge as something organized by subject divisions and tested for mastery, not just discovered empirically.
japanese_transmission: A classical-Chinese character present in Japan from the Nara period, carried in with legal and administrative vocabulary — 科 in the sense of 'article, clause, category' appears in the ritsuryō legal codes. It is familiar but never central in premodern Japanese usage. Its load-bearing arrival is Meiji: Nishi Amane and his contemporaries recruit 科 to coin 科学 (science) in the 1870s, reaching back to the imperial examination system's sense of 'knowledge organized by subject division' to render the Western concept. The coinage then transfers into Chinese via Liang Qichao and becomes universal in both languages.

gloss: Awakening as event — the heart-mind's direct, transformative recognition of its own nature; the charged goal of Rinzai koan practice.

components.meaning: heart-mind radical (vertical form of 心, 3 strokes) — locates the act of awakening in the cognitive-affective organ, not in an external revelation
I, oneself (7 strokes) — serves as phonetic (wú → wù by tonal shift) but the semantic resonance of self-recognition is hard to dismiss: awakening as the mind knowing itself

graphic_evolution.form_description: 忄 (heart-mind, left side) + 吾 (self, right side) — already a phono-semantic compound in classical Chinese
graphic_evolution.notes: The compound's structure is legible from its first appearances: the heart-mind (忄) encountering or recognizing the self (吾). Classical commentators on the character noted that 悟 differs from mere 知 (knowing) in that it implies a transformative recognition — not information acquired but a nature seen through.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 忄 (3 strokes, vertical left) beside 吾 (7 strokes, right) — 10 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The modern form preserves the phono-semantic composition clearly. 忄 as a radical appears in dozens of psycho-spiritual terms: 忘 (forget), 想 (think), 念 (attend/mindfulness), 慈 (compassion). 悟 among them names the radical shift from confused to clear functioning — the event all the others circle around.

doctrinal_notes: The standard Chan/Zen term for the event of awakening — the moment of direct recognition of one's original nature. Distinguished from 覚 (kaku), which implies gradual cultivation toward wakefulness, by its typically sudden register: 悟 is an event, not a process. The compound 大悟 (great awakening) marks particularly total or irreversible breaks through. In Rinzai practice, koan work aims to provoke 悟 as a breakthrough; in Sōtō practice, Dōgen is more cautious about the term, preferring 覚 or 証 (realization) to avoid attachment to awakening as a special state.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first-wave Chinese Buddhist vocabulary in the Nara period (710–794), where it appears in translations of Abhidharma and early Prajñāpāramitā texts. Its load-bearing use as the primary term for the awakening event belongs to the Chan transmission: Eisai brings Rinzai Chan to Japan in 1191, and Dōgen brings Sōtō in 1227. In both lineages 悟 becomes the charged term for what practice aims at, though the schools differ sharply about its nature — sudden revelation versus continuous practice-realization. The Rinzai koan curriculum is structured precisely as a technology for producing 悟.

gloss: Deed, vocation, karma — originally a ceremonial bell-stand, extended to assigned task, occupation, and finally to intentional action that conditions the stream of consciousness across moments and lifetimes.

components.meaning: undivided — originally a pictograph of a large notched board used to hang bells and drums in ritual orchestras; extended to deed, occupation, vocation through the idea of a task assigned to one

graphic_evolution.form_description: A notched or serrated board — the large wooden stand used in ritual orchestras to suspend percussion instruments
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete and institutional: the large ceremonial board from which bells and chimes were hung in royal and temple orchestras. The extension path is from physical object → the task performed there → organized activity → vocation → deed generally. By the Warring States period 業 had become the standard word for one's assigned work or occupation.

graphic_evolution.form_description: The notched board becomes more abstract — the sawtooth upper element over 木 (wood) is visible
graphic_evolution.notes: The seal form preserves the wooden structure. The 木 (wood/tree) component at the bottom reads as the material of the original ceremonial board, though it functions as a radical assignment rather than an active semantic element in later usage.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 13 strokes — a complex upper element (the notched board) over 木 (wood, 4 strokes)
graphic_evolution.notes: The modern form is not readily decomposable into transparent semantic parts — the oracle bone origin as a ceremonial stand has been entirely obscured. 業 is now read primarily through its uses: occupation (職業), industry (産業), course work (授業), and above all karma (業, ごう) in Buddhist contexts.

doctrinal_notes: The primary Sino-Japanese rendering of Sanskrit karman (intentional action, karma). Buddhist 業 covers three modes: bodily action (身業), verbal action (口業), and mental action (意業). Unlike the popular Western sense of karma as cosmic retributive justice, Buddhist 業 is a psychological-causal claim: intentional acts condition the stream of consciousness, shaping perception and response in ways that persist across moments and (in traditional framing) across lifetimes. The Baizhang wild-fox koan (Blue Cliff Record Case 2) makes 業 the pivot of a famous Chan argument: the question of whether an enlightened person is free from 業 or remains subject to it.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Chinese Buddhist translation in the Nara period. The compound 因果 (cause and effect, karma-result) becomes standard temple vocabulary from the earliest Buddhist institutions. The Kamakura period deepens 業's presence: Dōgen's Shōbōgenzō engages karma extensively, arguing against a mechanical retributive reading in favor of karma as the ongoing activity of Buddha-nature itself. In colloquial modern Japanese, 業 persists in 職業 (occupation), 授業 (lesson), 卒業 (graduation) — the concrete-task register of the original pictograph — while the Buddhist register (ごう, karmic consequence) survives in fixed phrases like 業が深い (carrying heavy karma).

gloss: Rightness, guiding principle — Confucian righteousness rooted in ritual propriety, extended into the suffix that names every committed doctrine.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 羊 (sheep) above 我 (a serrated blade) — a ritual weapon beneath a sacrificial sheep
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense is tied to sacrificial propriety: the correct, ritually fitting act. From 'ritually correct' the meaning extends to 'right conduct,' 'righteousness,' and eventually to the abstract 'principle' or 'meaning' that a thing ought to embody.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 13 strokes — 羊 above 我; the sacrificial reading is no longer visible in everyday use
graphic_evolution.notes: In Confucian ethics 義 is one of the cardinal virtues alongside 仁 (benevolence), standing for rightness as fidelity to principle even against self-interest. In 主義 it becomes the suffix for doctrines and -isms — a doctrine is the 'guiding right principle' around which a movement orients.

doctrinal_notes: In 主義 (-ism), 義 carries the sense of guiding principle or doctrine: a 主義 is the principle one takes as master. Thus 民主主義 is literally 'the doctrine that takes 民主 (rule by the people) as its guiding principle.' The Confucian moral freight of 義 as righteousness lingers faintly — an -ism in Japanese is not merely a system of thought but something one is committed to as right.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, arriving with the Nara-period absorption of the Confucian classics, where 義 is one of the cardinal virtues and a load-bearing ethical term from the beginning of Japanese literacy. Buddhist usage adds another register: 義 as 'meaning, doctrinal sense' appears throughout Heian and Kamakura sutra commentary, as in 教義 (teaching's meaning) and 義理 (doctrinal principle). Its Meiji-era repurposing systematizes 主義 as the suffix for Western -isms in the 1870s–80s, grafting Confucian moral weight onto the structure of doctrinal commitment imported from European political and philosophical vocabulary.

gloss: Seeing, discerning — the eye raised on legs, the whole body oriented toward perception; in 見性 the act of direct, breakthrough sight into one's own nature.

components.meaning: eye — the organ of perception, 5 strokes
legs of a person in motion, 2 strokes

graphic_evolution.form_description: A large eye (目) placed atop the legs of a walking figure (人) — the entire body oriented toward seeing
graphic_evolution.notes: The oracle bone form is immediately legible: a person whose defining activity is looking. The eye is large and prominent relative to the body, marking sight as the character's essential action. 見 differs from 目 (eye as organ) by adding the element of agency — a subject who is actively seeing, not merely a passive optical instrument.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 目 (eye, 5 strokes) over 儿 (legs, 2 strokes) — 7 strokes total; the compositional logic remains visible
graphic_evolution.notes: The modern form still shows the eye over the legs. 見 functions as both a standalone verb and as the radical for characters involving sight and visibility: 観 (kankan, to observe), 覚 (kaku, to wake/perceive), 親 (shin, parent — one who watches over). Its role in 見性 foregrounds the cognitive-perceptual register: kenshō is an act of seeing, not merely thinking or inferring.

doctrinal_notes: In 見性 (kenshō), 見 is not ordinary sensory sight but the act of direct, unmediated perception that penetrates to the nature of mind. Chan/Zen teachers repeatedly distinguish 見 (direct seeing) from 知 (conceptual knowing) and 解 (intellectual understanding): to see one's nature is not to form a concept about it. The character's oracle bone origin — the whole body of a person oriented toward sight — carries doctrinal weight: kenshō is embodied recognition, not disembodied cognition.
japanese_transmission: Present in Japan from the first-wave adoption of Chinese writing in the 5th–6th centuries, where it functioned as an ordinary verb of perception. Its consecrated Buddhist sense develops gradually: Nara-period Hossō scholasticism uses 見 in the sense of 'views' (見解, kenge) — the right and wrong views catalogued in Abhidharma psychology. The specific compound 見性 arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period; Eisai's Kōzen Gokokuron (1198) and Dōgen's Shōbōgenzō (1230s–1250s) both engage 見性 as a technical term, with Dōgen notably problematizing whether seeing one's nature can be parsed as subject seeing object. In ordinary modern Japanese 見る is the workaday verb for looking at anything — the contemplative register belongs to the classical Buddhist stratum.

gloss: Nature, inherent disposition — the life-force (生) of the heart-mind (忄); in Chan/Zen the intrinsic character of all things, identified directly with Buddha-nature.

components.meaning: heart-mind radical (vertical form of 心, 3 strokes) — locates nature in the cognitive-affective organ
life, birth, to arise (5 strokes) — the nature one is born with; the life-energy expressing through the heart-mind

graphic_evolution.form_description: 心 (heart) beside 生 (life/birth) — the inborn quality of the heart-mind, already a phono-semantic compound by the Qin period
graphic_evolution.notes: The combination of heart-mind and life/birth encodes the idea of inborn disposition: what the heart-mind is by nature, not what it becomes through cultivation. Classical Chinese philosophical usage distinguishes nature (性) from emotion (情) precisely on this axis — 性 is what you were born with; 情 is how it moves in response to circumstances.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 忄 (vertical heart-mind radical, 3 strokes) on the left beside 生 (life, 5 strokes) on the right — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplified left-side radical 忄 is the vertical form of 心, appearing in dozens of psycho-spiritual characters: 情 (emotion), 愛 (love), 悟 (awaken), 慈 (compassion). 性 among them names the underlying nature that all these movements express or obscure. In Buddhist usage 自性 (jishō, self-nature) and 仏性 (busshō, Buddha-nature) both use 性 to point at the innermost character of things.

doctrinal_notes: One of the most philosophically active characters in East Asian thought, carrying simultaneous Confucian (innate moral nature), Daoist (spontaneous natural character), and Buddhist (intrinsic nature of phenomena, Buddha-nature) registers. In Chan/Zen, 見性 ('seeing one's nature') names the breakthrough into this innermost character — not the nature of one individual self, but the Buddha-nature that all beings and phenomena share. Dōgen's radical rereading in the 'Busshō' fascicle proposes that all being is Buddha-nature (有仏性), not that all beings have Buddha-nature — a shift that repositions 性 from a property to an identity.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Chinese script adoption, where it carries the Confucian sense of innate moral nature — the term is central to Mencius's teaching on the goodness of human nature (性善説, seizen setsu). Buddhist registers layer on in the Nara period: Hossō Yogācāra distinguishes multiple types of nature (種性, shushō) corresponding to different paths of liberation. The specifically Chan/Zen load — 自性, 見性, 仏性 — arrives with the Kamakura transmission and becomes the primary register in which 性 is philosophically active in Japanese Zen discourse. The kun reading さが (innate character, fate, nature) preserves a distinctly Japanese nuance of disposition as something fated or characteristic of a type — a sense that surfaces in literary contexts more than doctrinal ones.

gloss: Phonetic vessel for Sanskrit bodhi — a grass canopy sheltering what reaches upward; a character defined by the sound it carries rather than the image it depicts.

components.meaning: grass/plant radical (3 strokes) — the top cover of the character, a conventional classifier
undivided below the radical — 者 provides the phonetic element; the full character is a phonetic loan for Sanskrit bud-/bodhi

graphic_evolution.form_description: 艹 (grass radical) over a phonetic component — assembled to render the sound of Sanskrit 'bo' in 菩提 (bodhi) and 菩薩 (bodhisattva)
graphic_evolution.notes: 菩 has no independent Chinese meaning; it was coined specifically to transliterate Sanskrit. The grass radical is a classifier convention, not a semantic clue. Buddhist translators working from Sanskrit or Central Asian originals in the Eastern Han (2nd century CE) and Six Dynasties periods assembled characters like 菩 precisely to fit the phonetic needs of Sanskrit terms that had no Chinese equivalents.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 艹 (3 strokes) over a complex phonetic body — 11 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In usage, 菩 never appears alone — it functions only as the first character of 菩提 (bodai, bodhi) or 菩薩 (bosatsu, bodhisattva). Its phonetic origin means the character's history is entirely the history of Buddhist textual transmission into Chinese, not native semantic development.

doctrinal_notes: 菩 is the first character of two of the most important terms in Mahāyāna Buddhism: 菩提 (bodai, bodhi — awakening) and 菩薩 (bosatsu, bodhisattva — awakening being). In both cases it renders the Sanskrit root bud- (to awaken). Because the character has no independent Chinese semantic content, its entire meaning is contextual — it points immediately and exclusively to the Sanskrit doctrinal vocabulary it was coined to carry.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist scriptural transmission in the Nara period (710–794). 菩薩 (bosatsu) is among the earliest Sanskrit-derived terms in Japanese Buddhism, appearing in 8th-century temple records and ritual texts. 菩提 arrives alongside it as the standard rendering of bodhi. Both compounds were fully integrated into Japanese Buddhist vocabulary before the Heian period (794–1185), and both remain in active use in all schools of Japanese Buddhism today. The kun reading is empty — 菩 has no native Japanese phonology because it was always already a foreign sound-loan.

gloss: To lift up, to present — a hand holding something aloft; in 菩提 it completes the phonetic rendering of Sanskrit bodhi, its sense of upholding resonating quietly with awakening's elevation.

components.meaning: hand radical (simplified form of 手, 3 strokes) — the semantic element: an act of the hand
to be, correct, right (9 strokes) — phonetic element, provides the tí sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: 手 (hand) beside 是 (phonetic) — a phono-semantic compound: the hand doing what 是 sounds like
graphic_evolution.notes: The primary meaning in non-Buddhist contexts is to lift, to carry by hand, to bring forward — as in 提案 (teian, proposal: bringing something forward), 提供 (teikyō, to offer: holding out for another). The combination of hand and the phonetic is standard seal-script composition.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 扌 (hand radical, 3 strokes) on the left beside 是 (9 strokes) — 12 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In non-Buddhist usage 提 appears in 提案 (proposal), 提供 (provision, offering), 前提 (premise, what is put forward first). In Buddhist usage it appears almost exclusively as the second character of 菩提 — phonetically completing the Sanskrit bodhi — and 提唱 (teishō, a Zen master's formal Dharma presentation: the act of holding up and presenting a teaching).

doctrinal_notes: In 菩提 (bodai, bodhi), 提 functions primarily as a phonetic complement. But its intrinsic meaning — upholding, presenting, lifting into view — carries a latent semantic resonance with the concept it helps render: bodhi as what the practice holds up and presents, or what one lifts toward. The compound 提唱 (teishō, formal Dharma presentation) shows this register directly: the master 提 (holds up, presents) the teaching.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the Buddhist scriptural transmission in the Nara period as part of the compound 菩提. Its independent meaning (to lift, carry, present) also enters Japanese in compounds like 前提 (zentei, premise) and 提案 (teian, proposal), which are classical Chinese coinages naturalized into Japanese. The on reading だい in 菩提 and てい in 提案/提供 preserve the two main phonetic registers of the character. In modern Japanese, 提 is common in formal and academic contexts through these compound terms.

gloss: Vastness, fullness — a person with arms spread wide, occupying the full span of space; in 大悟 the qualifier of realization so complete it leaves no remainder.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A person (人) with both arms extended horizontally — the full lateral span of a human body
graphic_evolution.notes: The oracle bone form is one of the most transparent pictographs in the writing system: a person making themselves as large as possible. The arms spread wide convey not merely size but fullness, the occupation of maximum space. This embodied sense of wholeness-through-extension carries into 大悟: the great awakening is not awakening-plus-something-else but awakening that has occupied its full span, leaving no contracted corner of delusion.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Three strokes — a horizontal stroke (arms), a diagonal left, a diagonal right (legs): one of the simplest characters in the script
graphic_evolution.notes: At three strokes, 大 is among the least complex characters in the writing system, yet it functions as a radical for 天 (heaven — a person under the sky), 夫 (husband — a full grown person), and 太 (great, extreme — 大 with an additional dot of excess). The simplicity of the character is itself legible: vastness, when truly depicted, needs few strokes.

doctrinal_notes: In 大悟 (daigo), 大 qualifies the awakening as thorough, complete, whole — contrasted with 小悟 (shōgo), partial or preliminary glimpses of insight. The qualifier does not mean the awakening is metaphysically greater but that it is structurally complete: no remainder of delusion from which to recover, no need for further consolidation. Chan/Zen literature also uses 大 in 大死 (daishi, the great death of the ego-self that precedes awakening) and 大疑 (daigi, the great doubt that fuels koan practice) — in each case 大 signals not merely quantity but a qualitative completeness that transforms the experience from ordinary to decisive.
japanese_transmission: One of the most ancient and frequent characters in Japanese writing — present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and carrying no specialized Buddhist introduction because it was already a general classical Chinese word for 'great, large' before Buddhism arrived. Its doctrinal uses in Zen (大悟, 大死, 大疑) are Kamakura-period arrivals via the Chan transmission, but they ride on a character so thoroughly embedded in Japanese that no special transmission was required. The prefix 大 (ō/dai) is extraordinarily productive in modern Japanese: 大学 (university), 大会 (major assembly), 大臣 (minister of state) — it retains the sense of something at full span.

gloss: Immediately, just-this — a kneeling figure beside a vessel of food, the moment of approaching to eat; in Buddhist usage the marker of immediate identity rather than mediated relation.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A kneeling person beside a raised food vessel — the moment of approaching to eat
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: a person kneeling at a meal, just about to eat. From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'immediately, just at this moment, right here' develops. Classical Chinese commentators emphasized that 即 names not merely temporal immediacy but a relationship of identity — 'just this and not other.'

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper element (vessel) over 卩 (kneeling figure radical) — 7 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 即 functions as the literary 'namely, that is to say' (すなわ.ち) and as the prefix marking immediate succession or identity: 即時 (sokuji, immediately), 即位 (sokui, accession), 即心是仏 (sokushin zebutsu, this very mind is Buddha).

doctrinal_notes: In Buddhist usage 即 carries the heaviest doctrinal weight in compounds asserting non-dual identity. 色即是空 (form is precisely emptiness) — the Heart Sutra's pivotal formula — uses 即 to assert that form and emptiness are not two related things but identically one. 即心是仏 (this very mind is Buddha) — the Mazu Daoyi formulation that became a Chan/Zen slogan — uses 即 to collapse the distance between the practitioner's actual functioning mind and Buddhahood. The 即 in these formulas is doing precise work: it refuses any mediating relation. Not 'mind is connected to Buddha' or 'mind leads to Buddha,' but mind is, just-this, Buddha. The character is the workhorse of non-dualist assertion in East Asian Buddhist vocabulary.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the Nara period and used in the standard Buddhist non-dualist formulas from the earliest stratum of Japanese Buddhist literature. Its load-bearing register belongs to Mahāyāna doctrinal vocabulary throughout, with the Chan/Zen slogans of the Kamakura period (即心是仏) reactivating its potency for the practical pedagogy of the Zen tradition.

gloss: Is, this, correct — the verb of identification and the demonstrative pointing at what is the case; in Buddhist non-dual formulas the copula that does the load-bearing identifying work.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 日 (sun) over 正 (correct, upright) — the sun standing straight, that which is correctly so
graphic_evolution.notes: The classical etymology connects 是 to the sun at its highest point, the moment of unmistakable position. From this the meanings 'correct, this very thing, is' develop. In classical Chinese 是 is the standard demonstrative ('this') and one of the standard copulas ('is').

graphic_evolution.form_description: 日 (sun, 4 strokes) over 疋 (variant of 正, 5 strokes) — 9 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 是 is rare outside of fixed phrases and Buddhist or classical contexts. It survives in 是非 (zehi, 'right and wrong' or 'by all means') and in the Buddhist non-dual formulas: 色即是空 (shiki soku ze kū, form is precisely emptiness), 即心是仏 (sokushin zebutsu, this very mind is Buddha).

doctrinal_notes: In the cardinal Buddhist non-dual formulas, 是 is the copula doing the identification. 色即是空 — form precisely is emptiness. 即心是仏 — this mind is Buddha. The character's function in these formulas is grammatical but doctrinally heavy: 是 is what makes the assertion an identity rather than a comparison or association. Read carefully, the 是 in these formulas refuses any reading that would treat the two terms it joins as separate entities being related. It says they are, just-this, the same.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with first-wave Buddhist transmission as part of the technical vocabulary for translating Mahāyāna sutras. The Heart Sutra's 色即是空 — the most chanted line in East Asian Buddhism — keeps 是 in active liturgical use across all Japanese Buddhist schools to the present day, even though the character is otherwise rare in modern Japanese.

gloss: Body, person — originally a pictograph of a pregnant woman in profile; the lived body as the site of practice and the locus of awakening, not its obstacle.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A pregnant woman in profile, the swollen abdomen clearly visible
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning is 'pregnant body' — the woman whose body has become the site of new life. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to: the body generally, oneself, one's person. The pictographic origin is among the more vivid in the writing system.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 7 strokes — a vertical structure preserving the curved shape of the original
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 身 covers the body, oneself, a person, and one's social position. In Chan/Zen vocabulary it appears in the load-bearing compound 身心 (body-mind) and in 身心脱落 (casting off body-mind), Dōgen's term for his pivotal awakening.

doctrinal_notes: In 身心一如 (shinjin ichinyo, body-mind as one) and 身心脱落 (shinjin datsuraku, casting off body-mind), 身 is paired with 心 (heart-mind) to name the unified field of embodied experience. The doctrinal point is to refuse a dualism between body and mind: practice happens in and as the embodied person, not in a disembodied consciousness that uses the body as its instrument. Dōgen's emphasis on zazen as the activity of the whole body-mind, not merely a mental discipline conducted while the body sits still, is built on this refusal.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese word み (mi, body, oneself) from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy. Its doctrinal weight in 身心 compounds belongs primarily to the Kamakura Zen transmission, in which Dōgen's writings make the body-mind unity a foundational principle.

gloss: One, single, unity — the most elemental stroke; in Buddhist usage the qualifier of unity, identity, and the single condition that holds across apparent multiplicity.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A single horizontal stroke
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplest character in the writing system: a single horizontal line. The semantic content is direct — the number one, oneness, unity. From this primitive sense the character extends to: the same, identical, single, undivided.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 1 stroke — a single horizontal line, unchanged from the oracle bone form
graphic_evolution.notes: Functionally one of the most common characters in Japanese, appearing in countless compounds: 一人 (hitori, one person), 一日 (ichinichi, one day), 一切 (issai, all), 一如 (ichinyo, oneness-suchness), 統一 (tōitsu, unification), 第一 (daiichi, first).

doctrinal_notes: In Buddhist compounds 一 carries the load of unity and non-duality. 一如 (ichinyo, oneness-suchness) names the single thusness that holds across apparent multiplicity. 一切 (issai, all, the totality) extends 'one' to mean 'one and the same throughout.' 身心一如 (body-mind as one) refuses the body-mind dualism by asserting their identity. The character is small but doctrinally heavy — every claim about non-duality in East Asian Buddhism is built on the work 一 does in compounds.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, the most common numeral and one of the most frequent characters overall in Japanese writing. Its doctrinal use in Buddhist compounds is integral to the vocabulary from the Nara period onward.

gloss: Self, oneself, from — originally a pictograph of the nose; in Chinese philosophical and Buddhist usage the root of self-reference and the marker of what arises spontaneously from a thing itself.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A stylized depiction of a nose viewed from the front
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning is 'nose,' and the original use of 自 to mean 'self' may derive from the gesture of pointing at one's nose to indicate oneself — a gesture still common in modern Chinese and Japanese culture. The semantic shift from 'nose' to 'self' is one of the more vivid in classical Chinese.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 6 strokes — a rectangular form with horizontal interior strokes, fully abstract from the original pictograph
graphic_evolution.notes: The 'nose' meaning has been entirely displaced by the 'self / from / by oneself' meanings. 自 is productive in compounds: 自分 (jibun, oneself), 自然 (shizen, nature, what is so of itself), 自性 (jishō, self-nature), 自由 (jiyū, freedom, 'from oneself'), 自我 (jiga, ego).

doctrinal_notes: In 自性 (jishō, self-nature), 自 carries the Sanskrit svabhāva that Madhyamaka Buddhism most insistently denies — the inherent, independent self-existence that things would need in order to exist apart from their causes. Nāgārjuna's argument is that nothing has 自性, because everything arises in dependence on conditions. In Tathāgatagarbha and Chan/Zen contexts the term is sometimes used positively — 'see one's self-nature, become Buddha' — creating a doctrinal tension that careful commentators have to manage. The character is also central to 自然 (shizen, what is so of itself, nature) and to the Chan/Zen vocabulary of spontaneity (自由, 自在 — freedom, self-mastery).
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese reflexive みずから (mizukara, by oneself) and the adverbial おのずから (onozukara, of itself, naturally). The Buddhist register of 自性 arrives with the Nara-period scholastic transmission. Its modern productivity is enormous — 自 is one of the most common characters in Japanese and appears in countless compounds across all registers.

gloss: Dharma — water flowing in its proper course; the Buddha's teaching, the order of phenomena, and the law of how things are, all carried in a single character.

components.meaning: water radical (3 strokes) — semantic element: a process flowing in its course, regulated by its nature
to go (5 strokes) — semantic and partially phonetic element: the going-out, the departure, what proceeds

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (water) beside an earlier complex element including 廌 (a mythical horned creature of justice) — water and the creature that judges
graphic_evolution.notes: The classical etymology connects 法 to a mythical tradition in which a horned beast was used in trials of justice — water (氵) representing the leveling principle, the beast representing the discernment of right from wrong. The character was simplified early in its history to drop the beast component, leaving the modern form. The semantic core: a regulated, principled order — what flows in its proper course, the way things go.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (3 strokes) on the left beside 去 (5 strokes) — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary classical Chinese 法 means 'law, statute, method, principle.' Buddhist translators recruited it to render Sanskrit dharma — the Buddha's teaching, the constitutive elements of phenomena, and the law of how things are — exploiting the polysemy of the original. In modern Japanese 法 covers law (法律), method (方法), and dharma (仏法), with the religious register marked by context.

doctrinal_notes: 法 is the East Asian rendering of Sanskrit dharma, one of the most polysemous and load-bearing terms in Buddhist vocabulary. Dharma covers: (1) the Buddha's teaching as a body of doctrine, (2) the elementary constituents of phenomenal experience as analyzed in Abhidharma psychology, (3) the law or principle of how things are, and (4) phenomenon or thing in the most general sense. 法 carries all four registers, with context determining which is foreground. In compounds: 仏法 (the Buddha's teaching), 諸法 (all phenomena), 法性 (the nature of dharmas), 法界 (the dharma-realm), 法身 (the dharma-body of a Buddha). Chan/Zen exploits the polysemy: 法 can be the formal teaching to be transmitted and simultaneously the bare reality of things prior to any teaching about them.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist transmission in the Nara period as the standard rendering of Sanskrit dharma. The native Japanese word のり (nori, law, principle) is mapped onto it as the kun reading. The character is among the most frequent in Japanese Buddhist vocabulary and appears in countless compounds across all schools. The non-Buddhist sense of 'law, method' also enters Japanese early and remains the dominant register in legal and technical contexts (法律 hōritsu, 方法 hōhō).

gloss: True, real, genuine — the unfeigned condition of a thing as it is, prior to ornamentation or distortion; in Buddhist usage the qualifier of reality as it is before conceptual elaboration.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A complex form combining elements possibly related to a vessel and observation
graphic_evolution.notes: The original etymology is disputed; Han-period commentators connected it to the image of an immortal or Daoist sage in their unaltered nature. The semantic core that survives is unambiguous: 真 names what is real and unfeigned, contrasted with 偽 (false, fabricated).

graphic_evolution.form_description: 10 strokes — a vertical structure with 目 (eye) embedded in the upper portion
graphic_evolution.notes: Used across Chinese and Japanese for 'true, real, genuine.' Productive in compounds: 真理 (shinri, truth), 真実 (shinjitsu, fact, truth), 真如 (shinnyo, suchness), 真心 (magokoro, sincere heart). The Buddhist register in 真如 is technical.

doctrinal_notes: In 真如 (shinnyo), 真 names reality as it is in itself, prior to and beyond conceptual elaboration. The character pairs with 如 (thusness, just-as-it-is) to render Sanskrit tathatā — the suchness of phenomena, their nature when not overlaid by the discriminating activity of the mind. 真 in this compound is doing limiting work: it specifies that the 'thusness' in question is not appearance but the actual reality, the true thusness as opposed to a mistaken or partial one.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese word まこと (sincerity, truth). Its Buddhist register develops with the Nara-period Yogācāra and Madhyamaka transmissions, in which 真如 (Sanskrit tathatā) becomes a load-bearing technical term. In modern Japanese 真 is one of the most common characters and carries a wide range of senses from doctrinal to colloquial.

gloss: Thusness, just-as — to be like, to correspond, to be in accordance; in Buddhist usage the suchness of phenomena, their condition just as they are without conceptual overlay.

components.meaning: woman radical (3 strokes) — semantic element with disputed origin in this character
mouth (3 strokes) — semantic element: a mouth speaking what corresponds, what is in accordance

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (woman) beside 口 (mouth) — semantic logic disputed
graphic_evolution.notes: Classical commentators offered various accounts: a woman speaking in accordance, or 女 as phonetic with 口 as semantic. The semantic core that survives is the sense of 'being like, being in accordance, just as.' From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'thusness' (the way a thing is) develops.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (3 strokes) beside 口 (3 strokes) — 6 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 如 functions as the literary 'just as, like' (ごと.し). Its load-bearing Buddhist use is in 真如 (shinnyo, suchness) and 如来 (nyorai, Tathāgata, 'thus-come' — the standard epithet of a Buddha). Both terms render Sanskrit tathā and its derivatives — the 'thus' that names reality as it is.

doctrinal_notes: In 真如 (shinnyo, suchness) and 如来 (nyorai, Tathāgata, 'thus-come one'), 如 renders Sanskrit tathā — the 'thus' that names things as they are without conceptual addition. The doctrinal point of 如 is its refusal to add anything: reality as it is requires no characterization beyond 'just thus.' The Tathāgata is one who has 'thus come' and 'thus gone' — one whose movement is in accordance with the suchness of things rather than against it. The compound 真如 doubles the emphasis: the true thusness, reality just as it is and not otherwise.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist scriptural transmission in the Nara period as part of the technical vocabulary for translating Sanskrit tathatā and tathāgata. The compounds 真如 and 如来 are both fully integrated into Japanese Buddhist usage from the earliest period and remain stable across all Japanese Buddhist schools. The kun reading ごと.し survives in literary and classical Japanese as the conjunction 'just as, like,' but the doctrinal register belongs to the Buddhist stratum.

gloss: To open — two hands lifting the bar from a closed gate; in 開悟 the verb of inception, naming the moment awakening first dawns as the opening of what was closed.

components.meaning: gate (8 strokes) — the semantic frame: doors of a closed gate, between which the action takes place
two hands lifting (4 strokes in simplified form) — the semantic element: hands raising the bar that holds the gate shut

graphic_evolution.form_description: 門 (gate) with an interior element depicting two hands and a bar — the act of removing the bar
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: hands lifting the bar from a closed gate. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to: opening generally, beginning, inaugurating, making accessible. The figural use in 開悟 (opening to awakening) preserves the structural sense — what was closed becomes accessible.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 門 (8 strokes) framing an interior element — 12 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 開 is widely productive: 開く (hiraku, to open), 開始 (kaishi, commencement), 開発 (kaihatsu, development), 開悟 (kaigo, opening to awakening). The doctrinal register in 開悟 is technical and confined to Buddhist usage.

doctrinal_notes: In 開悟 (kaigo, opening to awakening), 開 is the verb of inception. The character does not name the completeness of awakening — that is 大悟 — but the moment of opening, the breakthrough through which awakening first becomes accessible. The figural logic is consistent with 開 elsewhere: what was closed off becomes available, the gate is unbarred, the path is opened. In Chan/Zen contexts 開悟 tends to be used for the initial breakthrough moment rather than for the full and final realization, though the line between 開悟 and the more emphatic 大悟 is not always sharply drawn.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japan from the earliest stratum of literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese verbs ひらく (hiraku) and あく (aku). Its doctrinal use in 開悟 arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period and remains a stable but not dominant term in the Zen vocabulary of awakening — less prominent than 悟り or 大悟 but a recognized formulation. In ordinary modern Japanese 開 is one of the most common characters and carries no Buddhist resonance outside of fixed compounds.

gloss: To become, to complete — a halberd marking the moment a thing reaches its full form; in 成仏 the verb of attainment, becoming what one already structurally is.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 戈 (halberd) with a small mark — possibly indicating completion or settlement
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense is disputed. Some commentators read the halberd as marking the moment of victory or settlement of an affair; others see it as a phonetic component. The semantic core that survives into classical and modern usage is unambiguous: 成 names the moment a thing reaches its completed form.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 6 strokes — a complex form with 戈 (halberd) on the right side
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese, 成 is widely productive: 成る (naru, to become), 完成 (kansei, completion), 成功 (seikō, success), 成仏 (jōbutsu, becoming Buddha). Across these uses the underlying logic is consistent — a process reaching its fulfilled form.

doctrinal_notes: In 成仏 (jōbutsu, becoming Buddha) and 見性成仏 (kenshō jōbutsu, seeing one's nature, becoming Buddha), 成 is the verb of attainment. The doctrinal subtlety is that the becoming is not the production of something new but the manifestation of what was already structurally the case. Chan/Zen exploits this tension: 成仏 is what practice aims at and simultaneously what one already is — the becoming is not a transition between states but a recognition that requires no transition.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese verb なる (naru, to become) from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy. Its load-bearing Buddhist use in 成仏 arrives with the Nara-period Buddhist transmission and remains stable across all Japanese Buddhist schools — every tradition uses 成仏 as the standard verb for attaining Buddhahood. In modern colloquial Japanese 成仏 also functions as a euphemism for dying peacefully, with the implication that the deceased has reached liberation.

gloss: Buddha — a person (亻) marked as transcendent or extraordinary; phonetic transliteration of Sanskrit buddha, the awakened one, foundational to all Buddhist vocabulary in East Asia.

components.meaning: person radical (vertical form of 人, 2 strokes) — the semantic element: a Buddha is a kind of person, an awakened being
private, self (2 strokes) — phonetic element in the simplified form

graphic_evolution.form_description: Traditional 佛: 亻 (person) beside 弗 (phonetic, providing the fó sound) — assembled to render Sanskrit buddha
graphic_evolution.notes: The traditional form 佛 was coined specifically to transliterate Sanskrit buddha. The 亻 radical signals that the referent is a kind of person; 弗 provides the phonetic value. The character has no native Chinese semantic content prior to Buddhist usage — it was assembled from existing components to fit the doctrinal vocabulary.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Simplified to 仏 (4 strokes): 亻 (person) beside 厶 (a simple residual element)
graphic_evolution.notes: The Japanese postwar simplification (新字体, shinjitai) replaces 弗 with 厶, reducing the stroke count substantially. The simplified form is now universal in Japanese print. Mainland Chinese simplified script uses 佛 → 佛 (no change in PRC simplification, though traditional 佛 remains in Taiwan and Hong Kong).

doctrinal_notes: The single most important character in Buddhist vocabulary. 仏 is the Buddha — Śākyamuni in his historical reference, the awakened ones generally as a class, and the Buddha-nature in beings as a doctrinal claim. The character appears in countless compounds: 仏教 (Buddhism), 仏性 (Buddha-nature), 仏心 (Buddha-mind), 成仏 (becoming Buddha), 念仏 (recitation of the Buddha's name). Chan/Zen's distinctive contribution is the assertion that the Buddha is not elsewhere — not a deity to be supplicated, not a historical figure to be venerated from a distance, but the very functioning of one's own awakened nature: 即心是仏 (this very mind is Buddha).
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japan with the formal introduction of Buddhism in the 6th century CE — traditionally dated to 538 or 552 with the Korean Baekje court's gift of Buddhist images and texts to the Yamato court. The character is among the earliest Buddhist loans into Japanese and has been continuously in use since. The kun reading ほとけ (hotoke) is a native Japanese word with disputed etymology — possibly from 'hotoborkeshi' (one who has cooled passion) or from a Korean loan — that became the standard kun reading. In modern Japanese 仏 covers the full range of Buddhist meanings and also functions as a respectful term for the dead (ほとけさま, hotokesama), reflecting the cultural identification of death with attaining Buddhahood through 成仏.

gloss: Death — a person bowing beside the residue of bones; in Chan/Zen the figural ego-death (大死) that must precede genuine awakening, the dissolution of the seer-seen structure.

components.meaning: bone fragment, residue of death (4 strokes) — semantic element naming what is left after life departs
person bowed or kneeling (2 strokes) — semantic element: a figure in mourning posture

graphic_evolution.form_description: A bone fragment beside a kneeling figure — a person mourning beside the remains of the dead
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete and ritual: a living person in attendance on the residue of one who has died. The semantic logic carries: 死 names not the abstract event of dying but the relationship between the living and what death has left behind.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 歹 (bone fragment, 4 strokes) on the left beside 匕 (kneeling figure, 2 strokes) — 6 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The pictographic logic remains visible. 死 functions in everyday Japanese as the standard verb for dying (死ぬ shinu). Its doctrinal weight in Chan/Zen is concentrated in compounds like 大死 (daishi, the great death) and 生死 (shōji, birth-and-death) — in each case the term is used not to name biological death but the structural transformations of consciousness that the figure of death allows the tradition to point at.

doctrinal_notes: In Chan/Zen, 死 is used in two doctrinally important compounds. In 生死 (shōji), it pairs with 生 (birth) to name saṃsāra — the cycle of birth-and-death from which awakening liberates, but which Dōgen's Shōji fascicle insists is the very locus of liberation. In 大死 (daishi, the great death), 死 names the figural ego-death that must precede genuine awakening: the complete dissolution of the ordinary subject-object structure of consciousness, the death of the self that grasps at experience as its possession. The Linji school in particular emphasizes 大死 as the necessary condition for 大悟 — only one who has died the great death can be born into the great life that awakening reveals.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese verb しぬ (shinu, to die). Its doctrinal use in Chan/Zen compounds — 大死, 生死, 死活 — arrives with the Kamakura transmission and remains technical, confined to Buddhist contexts. In ordinary modern Japanese 死 is the workaday term for death and dying.

gloss: Beginning, inception — the moment a process initiates; in 始覚 the qualifier marking awakening as something arrived at by a particular practitioner at a particular time, contrasted with 本覚's structural priority.

components.meaning: woman radical (3 strokes) — semantic element with associations to origin and birth
platform, foundation (5 strokes) — phonetic element providing the shǐ sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (woman) beside 台 (phonetic) — semantic and phonetic elements
graphic_evolution.notes: Classical commentators glossed 始 as the moment a thing first comes into being — explicitly tied through the female radical to images of birth and origin. The semantic logic is straightforward: 始 names the first instant of any process, the inception point from which a thing extends.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (3 strokes) on the left beside 台 (5 strokes) — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern usage 始 is the workhorse character for 'begin, start': 始める (hajimeru, to start), 開始 (kaishi, commencement), 始末 (shimatsu, beginning and end of an affair). Its doctrinal weight in 始覚 is technical and almost entirely confined to Tendai and Zen contexts.

doctrinal_notes: In 始覚 (shikaku, acquired or initiated enlightenment), 始 marks the temporal arrival of awakening for a particular practitioner — the moment recognition first dawns, in contrast to 本覚 (hongaku) which marks awakening as the structural prior. The pair are not opposed positions but two moments of a single doctrine: 本覚 names what is realized; 始覚 names the realizing of it. The Awakening of Faith treatise systematizes this distinction, and Tendai theology elaborates it into a rich account of the relationship between intrinsic and acquired modes of awakening.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period as the standard term for 'beginning, inception.' Its doctrinal use in 始覚 arrives with the Heian Tendai transmission of the Awakening of Faith and its commentaries — Saichō and his successors elaborate the 本覚/始覚 distinction as a foundational theological frame. The doctrinal usage is technical and remains so; in ordinary modern Japanese 始 has no Buddhist resonance.

gloss: Root, origin, foundation — a tree (木) with a mark at its base; the original ground from which a thing extends, recruited in Buddhist usage to name what is intrinsically and originally the case.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 木 (tree) with a horizontal mark or thickening at its base — pointing to the root
graphic_evolution.notes: An indicative-character (指事) construction: the tree's root is marked rather than depicted as a separate object. The semantic logic is concrete and immediate — what is at the base of the tree is its root, its origin, the place it grows from.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 木 (4 strokes) with a horizontal mark crossing the central vertical at its base — 5 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The added stroke at the base remains visible in the modern form. From 'tree's root' the meaning extends to: origin, foundation, fundamental principle, the source. In Buddhist contexts 本 marks what is original, intrinsic, prior to elaboration — as in 本来 (originally), 本性 (original nature), 本心 (original mind), 本覚 (original enlightenment).

doctrinal_notes: In 本覚 (hongaku, original enlightenment), 本 carries the load of Tendai-derived doctrine that awakening is not acquired through practice but is the originally and always already realized condition of all beings. The character also pairs with 始 (acquired) in the doctrinal contrast 本覚/始覚, and stands at the head of many key Zen compounds: 本来面目 (original face), 本心 (original mind), 本性 (original nature). In each case 本 names not a temporal first but a structural prior — what is the case before delusion's elaboration ever began.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese word もと (root, origin, basis). Its doctrinal weight in Japanese Buddhism develops in two waves: first with the Heian-period transmission of Tendai (Saichō, early 9th century), which makes 本覚 a central theological category; and again with Kamakura Zen, where 本来面目 (original face) and related compounds carry Chan's claim that what practice realizes is what was always already the case. The character is also one of the most common in modern Japanese — used as the counter for long thin objects, as the word for 'book' (本), and in countless compounds — but its philosophical register remains marked and Buddhist.

gloss: Wakefulness, awareness — to wake from sleep, to come to perception; in Buddhist usage the standing condition of being awake, contrasted with the event-character of 悟.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper element related to 學 (learn) over 見 (see) — perception arising through learning, or seeing in the wakeful state
graphic_evolution.notes: Traditional 覺 is a complex character; the simplified 覚 (used in modern Japanese and simplified Chinese) preserves the 見 base. The semantic core: a wakeful mode of perception, contrasted with the dulled or sleeping mode.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper canopy element over 見 (see, 7 strokes) — 12 strokes total in the Japanese simplified form
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplified form preserves 見 at the bottom — wakefulness as a kind of seeing. In Buddhist usage 覚 covers both the act of waking up and the standing condition of being awake; it is thus a more processual and ongoing term than 悟, which names the discrete event of awakening.

doctrinal_notes: 覚 is the Sino-Japanese character most often used to render Sanskrit buddha (the awakened one) in its etymological aspect — a Buddha is one who has woken up. The compound 仏 (Buddha) originally transliterated the Sanskrit phonetically, but doctrinal commentary consistently glosses it through 覚. In Tendai and Zen, 覚 functions as a standing condition rather than an event: 本覚 (original wakefulness) and 始覚 (acquired wakefulness) are paired as the two doctrinal poles of how beings are related to their awakened nature. Dōgen often prefers 覚 over 悟 precisely because 覚 names a continuous condition rather than a one-time breakthrough — practice as the activity of an already-awake nature, not a striving toward a future event.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist scriptural translation in the Nara period, where it is the standard character for translating buddha as 'the awakened one' and for the verbal sense of waking up. Its doctrinal sophistication develops through Heian Tendai: Saichō's introduction of the 本覚思想 (hongaku shisō, original-enlightenment thought) makes 覚 a load-bearing term in Tendai theology, and from Tendai it passes into the later Kamakura schools — including Zen, where Dōgen engages it critically. In ordinary modern Japanese the character has a wide range of uses: 覚える (oboeru, to remember/learn), 覚醒 (kakusei, awakening from sleep), 感覚 (kankaku, sensation) — the doctrinal register survives in fixed Buddhist compounds.

gloss: Gradually, by degrees — water seeping in increments; in 漸悟 the structural opposite of 頓, marking awakening conceived as progressive deepening rather than instantaneous breakthrough.

components.meaning: water radical (3 strokes) — the semantic element: a process of incremental seeping or flowing
to cut (11 strokes) — phonetic element providing the jiàn sound, originally also semantic in the sense of cutting into stages

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (water) beside 斬 (phonetic) — water flowing or soaking in by stages
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense draws on the image of water soaking gradually into ground or rising slowly in a vessel — a process whose direction is unmistakable but whose progress is incremental. From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'gradually, by degrees' emerges in classical usage.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (3 strokes) on the left beside 斬 (11 strokes) — 14 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 漸 survives in formal compounds: 漸進 (zenshin, gradual progress), 漸増 (zenzō, gradual increase), 漸次 (zenji, by degrees). Its load-bearing doctrinal use is 漸悟 — paired structurally with 頓悟 in the Tang-dynasty Chan controversy and inherited as a conceptual frame in Japanese Zen.

doctrinal_notes: In 漸悟 (zengo), 漸 marks awakening as something approached through stages — the Northern school's position that purification of obscurations and the deepening of insight occur progressively. The character does not name slowness so much as cumulative direction: the practitioner advances by degrees toward a result that is not given all at once. The polemical opposition with 頓 in Chan historiography is so structurally rigid that 漸 in Buddhist contexts almost never appears without its sudden counterpart in the background.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japan as part of the general classical-Chinese vocabulary in the Nara period, where it carries the ordinary sense of 'gradually, step by step.' Its consequential Buddhist register is Kamakura: the 頓漸 (sudden-gradual) controversy reaches Japan with the Chan transmission, and 漸悟 enters Japanese Zen discourse as the structural foil against which 頓悟 is defined. Outside of doctrinal contexts, 漸 in modern Japanese is restricted to formal and literary compounds — it has no everyday colloquial life.

gloss: Sudden, abrupt, all-at-once — a head bowing abruptly to the ground; in 頓悟 the Southern school's defining qualifier, marking awakening as instantaneous rather than cumulative.

components.meaning: to assemble, to gather — phonetic element providing the dùn sound, 4 strokes
head, page — the semantic element: a head bowing or stopping abruptly, 9 strokes

graphic_evolution.form_description: 頁 (head) beside 屯 (phonetic) — the head component signals the abrupt bowing or stopping motion that gives 頓 its core meaning
graphic_evolution.notes: The primary classical meaning: to bow the head to the ground in salutation or submission, especially the abrupt full-body prostration of kowtow. Extended to: sudden, abrupt, all-at-once. The motion of bowing — the entire body's sudden shift from upright to prostrate — provides the image of instantaneous transformation.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 屯 (4 strokes) on the left beside 頁 (head/page radical, 9 strokes) — 13 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: 頁 as a radical appears in characters involving the head: 頭 (head), 顔 (face), 願 (to wish/vow — bowing the head in prayer). 頓 among them names the abrupt movement of the head — and by extension any abrupt, all-at-once quality. The contrast with 漸 (gradual, step-by-step) is structurally built in: 頓悟 vs 漸悟 is the defining fault-line of Tang-dynasty Chan.

doctrinal_notes: In 頓悟 (tongo), 頓 marks the Southern school's doctrinal claim that genuine awakening arrives instantaneously — not at the end of a cumulative path of purification but as a single breakthrough that is complete in itself. The opposing term is 漸悟 (zengo, gradual awakening), associated with the Northern school's teaching of progressive purification. The Platform Sutra presents Huineng's sudden-awakening teaching as the authentic Chan transmission precisely against the gradual approach: 頓 is not mere quickness but a claim about the structure of awakening itself — it does not arrive in stages because there is no gap between recognizing one's nature and being it.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period (late 12th–13th centuries), embedded in the vocabulary of the Platform Sutra and the Rinzai koan curriculum. The 頓漸 (tonzen, sudden-gradual) debate was a live doctrinal controversy in Tang-dynasty China and remains a conceptual framework in Japanese Zen: Rinzai tends to foreground 頓 (sudden breakthrough through koan), while Sōtō tends to sidestep the contrast through shikantaza (just sitting), which Dōgen frames as practice-realization rather than either sudden or gradual. In modern Japanese, 頓 survives primarily in set compounds: 頓悟 (sudden awakening), 頓死 (sudden death), 頓智 (quick wit, quick-wittedness) — all carrying the sense of an abrupt, complete event.
